lines.text: Introduction, part three. How to use this book.
Play Optimal Poker is built around a series of scenarios, each one designed to illuminate specific concepts in a focused and digestible way.
The book begins not with full poker hands but with what are called toy games — simplified games that share some structural features with poker but strip away most of the complexity.
Toy games are easy enough to solve completely, which means you can study them without getting lost in the competing priorities that arise in real poker situations.
Each scenario in the book adds a new layer of complexity on top of what came before, so that you can see how all the individual pieces fit together as you progress.
Eventually, the book works its way up from these abstract toy games to applying the lessons directly to real poker situations you will actually encounter.
The way each scenario is structured is important.
Before presenting the solution, the book prompts you to make your own predictions about what the optimal strategies should be.
Some of these prediction questions will be straightforward, and others will be genuinely challenging.

Brokos recommends not banging your head against the wall trying to arrive at a perfectly precise answer before reading on, but he does strongly encourage you to spend some real time thinking independently before you look at the explanation.

The reason is simple: you learn and retain information much more effectively when you first engage with a problem yourself, even if you get it wrong, than when you simply read a solution handed to you.
The scenarios build on each other in a cumulative way, which means it matters that you genuinely understand the central concepts from each chapter before moving on to the next.
To help with this, each scenario includes overview sections and challenge questions that highlight the main takeaways and test whether you have actually absorbed them.
A good rule of thumb is to review those questions before you move on and make sure you can answer each one comfortably.
If you cannot, that is a signal to revisit the material rather than push forward.
Brokos also acknowledges that some readers may already have a solid background in game theory.
If that describes you, the early scenarios might feel very familiar.

The prediction questions at the start of each section are an opportunity to test how well you really know the material — and Brokos notes that even people with meaningful game theory knowledge sometimes get these details wrong.
He admits that in writing the book, he discovered gaps in his own understanding of concepts he thought he had fully mastered.
So if the prediction questions are easy and you get them right, feel free to skim or skip the rest of that section and move ahead.
But do check yourself honestly first.
After working through the equilibrium solution for each scenario, the book shifts to the exploitative angle.
This means thinking about the kinds of mistakes a player might realistically make in that situation and then figuring out how to best take advantage of each type of error.

Understanding the equilibrium is a valuable first step even when your actual goal is to play exploitatively, because knowing what the optimal baseline looks like makes it much easier to spot when an opponent is deviating from it.
The scenarios throughout the book feature two recurring players named Ivan and Opal.
Ivan is always the player who is in position — meaning he acts after his opponent on the betting rounds after the flop.
Opal is always out of position — she acts first.
In much of the academic and technical game theory literature, these roles are simply labeled I P and O P.
Brokos chose human names starting with those same letters because he believes they make the scenarios easier to visualize and follow.
Most of the scenarios involve just these two players, and that is intentional.
The lessons are most directly applicable to heads-up pots — just two players contesting the same hand.
In practice, even at a table with nine or ten players, many hands come down to exactly two players by the time the river is dealt, so the material is highly relevant to real games.

The general principles that emerge from two-player game theory analysis also mostly carry over to multi-way pots, even though the precise mathematics may be somewhat different when three or more players are involved.
On the subject of mathematics: yes, this book does involve some math.
But Brokos urges you not to get intimidated or bogged down by the numbers.
The exact numerical details are rarely the point.

What matters is understanding the concepts at a deeper level — grasping the relationship between how large a bet is and how often you need to bluff, for instance, or understanding why certain hands make better bluffing candidates than others.
Focus on the underlying logic, and let the specific numbers serve as illustrations rather than things to memorize.
Finally, Brokos is clear that these scenarios are meant to be engaging and useful, not purely theoretical exercises.
He has taken care to connect each abstract scenario back to the kinds of concrete decisions you actually face at a poker table.
At the end of the day, the instruction is the same as it is for game theory itself: take what is useful to you and leave behind what is not.
This book is another tool in your toolbox — a powerful one, built to make you a sharper, more confident, and more adaptable poker player.
